title: The small kindness you remember decades later
summary: Describe a small act of kindness from your past that seemed minor at the time but stayed with you for years, and reflect on why small gestures can have lasting effects.
topic: The small kindness you remember decades later
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) describing a small act of kindness you received that you still remember clearly, even though it happened a long time ago. Describe the moment in detail, explain why it mattered so much to you at the time, and reflect on what it taught you about how people affect each other. Use past simple to narrate events, at least one concession with 'although' or 'even though', and at least one modal verb.
hintStarters: The person who showed me the kindness probably forgot about it before the day was over, but I…
Although it sounds like nothing when I describe it, at the time it changed the entire…
I must have been about twelve years old when a stranger did something that I have never forgotten…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một người lạ đi ngang qua
cảm thấy lạc lõng, không thuộc về nơi đó
giữ cửa lại cho người khác

hintVocab.phrase: to be on the verge of tears
hintVocab.meaningVi: suýt khóc, gần rơi nước mắt
thừa nhận sự hiện diện của ai đó, chú ý đến ai đó
làm điều tốt cho người khác để đền đáp điều tốt mình nhận được

sampleText:
I was fourteen and had just started at a new school in Kuala Lumpur, three weeks after my family moved from Penang. I did not know anyone. At lunch on my second day, I picked up my tray and could not find anywhere to sit — every table seemed already claimed. A girl I had never spoken to looked up, moved her bag off the bench beside her, and said, 'Here.' That was all. She did not ask my name or explain anything. She just made room for me and then went back to her own conversation.

Although the whole thing lasted about ten seconds and she probably forgot it before the afternoon was over, I was on the verge of tears when I sat down. I had been telling myself all week that it did not matter, that I would find my footing eventually, but I had been struggling more than I admitted. That one small gesture — moving a bag — told me that I was not invisible. Someone had noticed I was standing there. It cost her nothing, but to me it felt like being acknowledged by the entire room. Small kindnesses often carry this kind of disproportionate weight when you are the person who needs them most.

What I took from that ordinary moment, and have tried to carry forward ever since, is that you rarely know which of your actions will stick in someone else's memory. The girl at that table would never have guessed that I still think about her. She could not have known that her brief, wordless gesture would stay with me through secondary school, through university in another country, and into adulthood. When I see someone looking lost in a crowded place — on a bus in Seoul, in a canteen in London, in a market in Cairo — I think of her. I try to make room. It costs nothing, and you can never tell when it might be exactly what someone needed.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: mọi bàn dường như đã bị chiếm rồi

sampleAnnotations.phrase: looked up, moved her bag off the bench
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: ngẩng đầu lên, dịch túi khỏi ghế băng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: went back to her own conversation
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: quay lại cuộc trò chuyện của riêng cô ấy

sampleAnnotations.phrase: was on the verge of tears

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tìm được chỗ đứng của mình
gặp khó khăn nhiều hơn tôi thừa nhận

sampleAnnotations.phrase: told me that I was not invisible
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: cho tôi biết rằng tôi không vô hình
được cả phòng chú ý đến
trọng lượng không tương xứng, quá lớn so với bề ngoài
in sâu vào trí nhớ của người khác
cử chỉ ngắn ngủi, không lời

sampleAnnotations.phrase: you can never tell when it might be exactly what someone needed
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: bạn không bao giờ biết khi nào điều đó có thể chính xác là thứ ai đó cần

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past continuous for interrupted background state
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had been telling myself all week that it did not matter' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn để cho thấy một suy nghĩ liên tục xảy ra trong suốt cả tuần trước thời điểm trong câu chuyện. Nó nhấn mạnh tính lặp lại và kéo dài của hành động.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had been telling myself all week that it did not matter' uses the past perfect continuous to show a thought that was happening repeatedly throughout the week before the story moment. It emphasises the ongoing, repeated nature of the action.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'would' for reporting past predictions
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'She could not have known that her brief, wordless gesture would stay with me' dùng 'would + base verb' trong văn phong tường thuật để chỉ tương lai nhìn từ một điểm trong quá khứ (future in the past). Đây là cách phổ biến để nói về kết quả lâu dài khi kể chuyện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'She could not have known that her brief, wordless gesture would stay with me' uses 'would + base verb' in reported speech to indicate a future event seen from a past point (future in the past). This is a common B1 structure for describing long-term outcomes in narratives.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi mười bốn tuổi và vừa bắt đầu ở một ngôi trường mới tại Kuala Lumpur, ba tuần sau khi gia đình tôi chuyển từ Penang. Tôi không biết ai cả. Vào bữa trưa ngày thứ hai, tôi cầm khay và không tìm được chỗ ngồi — mọi bàn dường như đã bị chiếm rồi. Một cô gái tôi chưa bao giờ nói chuyện ngẩng đầu lên, dịch túi khỏi ghế băng cạnh cô ấy, và nói: 'Đây nhé.' Chỉ vậy thôi. Cô ấy không hỏi tên tôi hay giải thích gì. Cô ấy chỉ nhường chỗ cho tôi rồi quay lại cuộc trò chuyện của riêng mình.

Mặc dù toàn bộ sự việc kéo dài khoảng mười giây và cô ấy có lẽ đã quên trước khi buổi chiều kết thúc, tôi suýt khóc khi ngồi xuống. Tôi đã tự nhủ cả tuần rằng không sao, rằng cuối cùng tôi sẽ tìm được chỗ đứng, nhưng tôi đang gặp khó khăn nhiều hơn tôi thừa nhận. Một cử chỉ nhỏ đó — dịch một cái túi — cho tôi biết rằng tôi không vô hình. Ai đó đã nhận ra tôi đang đứng đó. Điều đó không tốn gì của cô ấy, nhưng với tôi nó cảm giác như được cả phòng chú ý đến. Những sự tử tế nhỏ thường mang trọng lượng không tương xứng như thế này khi bạn là người cần chúng nhất.

Điều tôi học được từ khoảnh khắc bình thường đó, và đã cố gắng mang theo về sau, là bạn hiếm khi biết hành động nào của mình sẽ in sâu vào trí nhớ của người khác. Cô gái ở bàn đó sẽ không bao giờ đoán được rằng tôi vẫn nghĩ về cô ấy. Cô ấy không thể biết cử chỉ ngắn ngủi, không lời của mình sẽ theo tôi qua trung học, qua đại học ở một đất nước khác, và đến tuổi trưởng thành. Khi tôi thấy ai đó trông lạc lõng ở một nơi đông đúc — trên xe buýt ở Seoul, trong căng tin ở London, trong chợ ở Cairo — tôi nghĩ đến cô ấy. Tôi cố nhường chỗ. Điều đó không tốn gì, và bạn không bao giờ biết khi nào điều đó có thể chính xác là thứ ai đó cần.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect continuous correctly for an ongoing past action?
options: I had been telling myself all week that it did not matter.
I have been telling myself all week that it did not matter.
I was telling myself all week that it did not matter.
I had told myself all week that it does not matter.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect continuous ('had been telling') shows an action that was ongoing throughout a period before a specific past moment. 'All week' marks the duration.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect continuous = 'had been + -ing'. Use for ongoing actions before a past story moment.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'although' to introduce a concession?
options: Although the whole thing lasted about ten seconds, I was on the verge of tears.
Although the whole thing lasted about ten seconds, but I was on the verge of tears.
Although, the whole thing lasted about ten seconds, I was on the verge of tears.
The whole thing lasted about ten seconds, although I was still on the verge of tears too.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'although', no 'but' before the main clause.
mcQuestions.hint: No comma after 'although'; no 'but' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'could not have known' correctly for a past impossibility?
options: She could not have known that her gesture would stay with me.
She could not know that her gesture would stay with me.
She could have not known that her gesture would stay with me.
She cannot have known that her gesture would stay with me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could not have + past participle' expresses that something was impossible to know in the past. 'Cannot have known' is used for present deduction, not past.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Could not have + past participle' = it was impossible in the past. 'Cannot have' = present deduction.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'would + base verb' for future in the past?
options: I still think her gesture would stay with me through university and into adulthood.
Her gesture would stay with me through university and into adulthood.
Her gesture will stay with me through university and into adulthood.
Her gesture stays with me through university and into adulthood.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would + base verb' expresses a future event seen from a past point in time (future in the past). This is often used in narratives when describing what turned out to happen.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Would + base verb' in narrative = future from the perspective of a past moment.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'make room for' correctly?
options: She made room for me and then went back to her own conversation.
She made a room for me and then went back to her own conversation.
She made room to me and then went back to her own conversation.
She made the room for me and then went back to her own conversation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Make room for' is a fixed phrase — no article before 'room', and the preposition is 'for'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Make room for someone' — no article, preposition 'for'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'feel like + gerund or noun phrase'?
options: It felt like being acknowledged by the entire room.
It felt like to be acknowledged by the entire room.
It felt like I acknowledged by the entire room.
It felt as acknowledged by the entire room.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Feel like + gerund/noun phrase' is the correct structure. 'It felt like being acknowledged' uses a gerund after 'like'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Feel like' is followed by a gerund or noun phrase, not 'to + infinitive'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'rarely + auxiliary' correctly for a general truth?
options: You rarely know which of your actions will stick in someone else's memory.
You rarely knowing which of your actions will stick in someone else's memory.
You rarely are knowing which of your actions will stick in someone else's memory.
You rarely have known which of your actions will stick in someone else's memory.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Rarely' is a frequency adverb. It goes before the main verb in present simple: 'you rarely know'.
mcQuestions.hint: Frequency adverbs like 'rarely' go before the main verb in present simple.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'on the verge of' + gerund?
options: I was on the verge of tears when I sat down.
I was on the verge to cry when I sat down.
I was on the verge of to cry when I sat down.
I was on the verge crying when I sat down.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On the verge of + noun/gerund'. 'On the verge of tears' uses a noun; 'on the verge of crying' uses a gerund. Both are correct. 'On the verge to cry' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: 'On the verge of' is followed by a noun or gerund (-ing), not 'to + infinitive'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'pay attention to' in a narrative?
options: I try to pay attention to people who look lost in crowded places.
I try to pay attention at people who look lost in crowded places.
I try paying attention to people who look lost in crowded places.
I try to pay attention for people who look lost in crowded places.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Pay attention to' — the preposition is 'to', not 'at' or 'for'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'you can never tell' correctly to express uncertainty?
options: You can never tell when it might be exactly what someone needed.
You can never tell when it might be exactly what someone needs.
You never can tell when it might be exactly what someone needed.
You can never to tell when it might be exactly what someone needed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Can never tell' — the adverb 'never' goes between 'can' and the verb. 'Can never tell + when + clause' is natural English for expressing that something is unknowable.
mcQuestions.hint: Adverbs like 'never' go between the modal and the main verb: 'can never tell'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'cost + indirect object' for something free?
options: It cost her nothing, but to me it felt enormous.
It costed her nothing, but to me it felt enormous.
It has costed her nothing, but to me it felt enormous.
It cost to her nothing, but to me it felt enormous.
mcQuestions.explanation: Irregular past tense: 'cost → cost' (not 'costed'). Structure: 'cost + indirect object + amount': 'cost her nothing'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Cost' is irregular: past tense is also 'cost', not 'costed'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do small kindnesses sometimes affect us more deeply than grand gestures? Does the size of an act determine its importance, or is it something else — timing, context, or the state of the person receiving it?
